title: Towards a responsible AI adoption/adaptation (RAA) ecosystem: vision and model to keep socio-technological balance
abstract: The rapid adoption of AI technologies is outpacing our ability to assess their long-term societal and economic impacts. Initially, AI was expected to automate only repetitive, low-skill tasks. However, presently, highly skilled roles such as in software development, manufacturing, and finance are being automated. Tasks that once required many professionals can now be managed by a few, disproportionately benefiting those with greater resources and economic power, such as large corporations. This trend may lead to a growing socio-technological imbalance, for instance, a growing mismatch between the rapid advancements in artificial intelligence and society’s ability to adapt to and govern these changes in a fair, ethical, and inclusive manner. As AI-driven automation is increasingly being adopted across almost all business domains, covering all process-level functions. Additionally, there is a lack of practical methods for the ethical and responsible adoption of AI, which are either not being implemented or not well understood by stakeholders in the business ecosystem. This emphasizes the role of Responsible AI (RAI), which is becoming critical and essential in addressing the socio-technological imbalance in the organizational context regarding adopting/adapting AI. RAI ensures that systems are developed and deployed with core principles such as explainability, fairness, ethics, transparency, accountability, human oversight, and privacy. Furthermore, AI itself presents as a technological capability to promote responsible behavior by assessing, predicting, supporting, and regulating its own societal (i.e., organization-specific) and technological impacts. This study explores this dual perspective by reviewing the literature to propose a RAI Adoption/Adaptation (RAA) framework, focusing on the business process as the primary unit of AI intervention. A conceptual system implementation context is proposed to illustrate RAA enablement at the process level and highlight the need for an RAI agentic architecture capable of measuring, analyzing, and forecasting AI’s impact on business processes to support ethical, balanced innovation. © The Author(s) 2026.
prior_rationale: Responsible AI adoption framework explicitly covers software development automation and proposes an agentic RAI architecture for measuring AI business-process impact, but scope is too broad to confirm direct code governance applicability from the abstract.

title: Generative AI in responsible conversational agent integration: guidelines for service managers
abstract: Responsible integration of conversational agents (CAs) like chatbots is crucial for service firms to mitigate risks and foster positive outcomes. This article provides managerial guidelines through a Corporate Digital Responsibility (CDR) lens, focusing on CDR Culture, Management Structure, and Digital Governance across the service firm, software provider, and customers/society. It examines how organizational sensemaking processes of creation, interpretation, and enactment are triggered by CA-related issues and events. The research highlights the role of generative AI (GenAI) in implementing CDR factors and responsible CA software development lifecycle phases during development and integration. Guidelines are provided for leveraging GenAI to enhance CDR Culture, incorporate ethical considerations into CDR Management Structure, and enable robust Digital Governance mechanisms to prioritize customer/societal well-being. A multilevel framework illustrates reinforcing the guidelines through organizational sensemaking processes, and fostering responsible CA integration aligned with ethical principles and societal values. © 2024 The Authors
prior_rationale: Responsible CA integration guidelines include a software provider perspective and governance mechanisms across the development lifecycle, but the conversational agent domain requires translation argument to apply to AI code generation governance.

title: Transforming LLMs into domain experts: a case study of train trajectory evaluation
abstract: Traditional train trajectory evaluation methods suffer from subjectivity, inefficiency, and poor adaptability to dynamic conditions. This study proposes a tuning-free LLM framework that integrates domain knowledge through dynamic prompts and human-in-the-loop feedback, enabling automated code generation for real-time driver assessment. This framework contains three main parts: constraint-driven prompt engineering dynamically generates structured prompts by integrating task semantics and domain knowledge, and a modular code synthesis produces executable code via stepwise generation, separating data processing, feature calculation, and rule application into modular components while embedding domain-specific constraints. Furthermore, a human-in-the-loop optimization workflow is introduced to validate outputs through automated execution and human feedback, refining prompts and code iteratively until convergence. This study bridges LLMs' linguistic capabilities with domain-specific problem-solving, achieving an average of 85.9% consistency with numerical simulations while significantly reducing expert dependency.  © 2025 IEEE.
prior_rationale: HITL workflow that validates LLM-generated code through automated execution and iterative human feedback is structurally parallel to AI code review; domain (railway driver assessment) requires translation but the oversight mechanism itself is transferable.

title: Bias in generative AI systems: a 3-layer response and liability determination
abstract: The risk of bias is widely noticed in the entire process of generative artificial intelligence (generative AI) systems. To protect the rights of the public and improve the effectiveness of AI regulations, feasible measures to address the bias problem in the context of large data should be proposed as soon as possible. Since bias originates in every part and various aspects of AI product lifecycles, laws and technical measures should consider each of these layers and take different causes of bias into account, from data training, modeling, and application design. The Interim Measures for the Administration of Generative AI Service (the Interim Measures), formulated by the Office of the Central Cyberspace Affairs Commission (CAC) and other departments have taken the initiatives to govern AI. However, it lacks specific details on issues such as how to prevent the risk of bias and reduce the effect of bias in decision-making. The Interim Measures also fail to take causes of bias into account, and several principles must be further interpreted. Meanwhile, regulations on generative AI at the global level are still in their early stages. By forming a governance framework, this paper could provide the community with useful experiences and play a leading role. The framework includes at least three parts: first, determining the realm of governance and unifying related concepts; second, developing measures for different layers to identify the causes and specific aspects of bias; third, identifying parties with the skills to take responsibility for detecting bias intrusions and proposing a program for the allocation of liabilities among the large-scale platform developers. © 2024 Sichuan Academy of Social Sciences. All rights reserved.
prior_rationale: Three-layer bias governance framework (data, modeling, application) with liability allocation for generative AI could transfer to code generation oversight architecture, though the focus on bias/fairness rather than code quality or security limits direct applicability.

title: Human-centred research automation: agentic AI framework for literature discovery, filtering, and gap identification
abstract: Recent advances in agentic AI raise opportunities to automate complex research tasks; however, the EU AI Act and Human-Centred AI (HCAI) principles emphasise that such automation must safeguard trust, transparency, and responsibility. This work-in-progress study developed an automated framework using agentic AI to support SLRs, which are critical for synthesising knowledge and identifying research gaps but remain resource-intensive, time-consuming, and in need of trust and transparency in their processes. The study presents a human-centred, agentic AI framework for discovering, filtering, analysing academic papers, and identifying research gaps. The proposed framework combines machine efficiency with human oversight to ensure contextual interpretation and responsible judgment. In a pilot study using Llama 3.1 8b, it achieved 71.05% accuracy for abstract filtering compared to a human-led SLR baseline. It also generates structured outputs, such as research gaps, potential directions, and candidate solutions, that enhance clarity and evaluability of the automated synthesis. Preliminary findings demonstrate that automation makes the initial synthesis faster but the human involvement in the later stage ensures that the result can be reliable and transparent. To further assess the framework’s alignment with HCAI principles, the next phase will conduct qualitative researcher interviews to evaluate perception of trust and transparency, as well as measuring how structured outputs improve interpretability. Overall, the study contributes to the design of responsible and trustworthy AI systems for research, demonstrating how agentic AI can make SLRs more efficient, reliable, and accessible, while advancing discussions at the intersection of agentic AI, systematic reviews, and human-centred research practice.
prior_rationale: Human-centred agentic AI framework with measured HITL accuracy (71%) for high-volume filtering has structural parallels to code review scaling but domain translation from academic literature to code review is non-trivial.
